claims.text: In 1991 German historian Heribert Illig and colleagues proposed the Phantom Time Hypothesis: the years 614 through 911 AD never happened — they were inserted into the calendar around 1000 AD by a conspiracy between Holy Roman Emperor Otto III, Pope Sylvester II, and the Byzantine emperor to relocate themselves into the new millennium.

claims.rationale: The Phantom Time Hypothesis is a real proposal by Heribert Illig published in 1991 in 'Das erfundene Mittelalter'. The host accurately reports its core claim. The hypothesis itself is universally rejected by professional historians; directTruth here scores the description-of-the-hypothesis as accurate (~0.4 hedged because the host conflates it with the related Hans-Ulrich Niemitz / Otto III conspiracy details).

claims.text: Pope Gregory's 1582 calendar reform removed only 10 days when accumulated Julian-calendar drift since 45 BC should have required 13 — and that 3-day discrepancy is suspicious circumstantial evidence that ~300 years had been illegitimately inserted into the historical record after Gregory's reference point.

claims.rationale: The math is correctly described: Julian drift was about a day per 128 years and 1582-(-45) = 1627 years implies ~12.7 days. But the host's later debunking is correct — Gregory was correcting the calendar back to the Council of Nicaea reference (325 AD), not to Caesar's 45 BC adoption, so 1582-325 = 1257 years × 1 day/128 = ~9.8 days, which rounds to 10. The '3-day discrepancy means a conspiracy' inference is the Illig argument that the host's verdict section dismantles.

claims.text: Illig argues that there is essentially no surviving European architecture, technical advancement, or written record from 614 to 911 — castles built before 600 and after 1000 are abundant, but nothing from the gap; year-476 architecture is virtually identical to year-1000 architecture, with three centuries of zero progress in between.

claims.rationale: This is Illig's empirical claim, but it is wildly wrong on the facts. The Anglo-Saxon, Frankish, Lombard, and Byzantine kingdoms all left abundant 7th-9th-century architecture (Hagia Sophia in its current form is 6th c.; the Great Mosque of Córdoba began 784; the Aachen Palatine Chapel 792; Sutton Hoo finds c.625; etc.). The 'no castles' point also misframes — the European stone-castle form proper postdates 1000 even on mainstream chronology, replacing earlier wooden motte-and-bailey structures.

claims.text: A 1986 archaeological survey in Germany found essentially zero medieval-town discoveries attributable to the period 650-910 AD — supporting the Phantom Time claim that nothing was actually happening in those centuries because they didn't exist.

claims.rationale: There is no such 1986 archaeological survey supporting Illig's gap. German medieval archaeology of the 7th-10th centuries is well populated — Carolingian palaces, monasteries (Reichenau, Lorsch, Fulda), Birka and Hedeby trade-emporia, and abundant grave goods. Illig's selective evidence-of-absence claim does not survive contact with the actual archaeological record.

claims.text: Charlemagne's biography is biographically impossible — 46-year reign with 44 years at war while simultaneously creating a jury system, 100+ laws, education reform, and updating scripture would take 4-5 lifetimes — supporting Illig's claim that Charlemagne was an Ottonian fabrication built to legitimize the Ottonian dynasty's grip on the Holy Roman Empire.

claims.rationale: Charlemagne is one of the best-documented figures of his era, with extensive contemporary biographies (Einhard, Notker the Stammerer), letters, capitularies, coinage, and physical remains in Aachen. His 'too many accomplishments for one man' framing is debunked in the host's verdict section: most achievements were collective (Alcuin and the Carolingian Renaissance scholars). The Ottonian-fabrication thesis has no documentary support.

dependencies.rationale: Charlemagne-as-fabrication is the central piece of the Phantom Time conspiracy.

claims.text: Records of Halley's Comet (which returns every 75-76 years) survive in Chinese chronicles continuously through the alleged 614-911 phantom centuries, providing an independent astronomical chronology that disproves the missing-three-centuries hypothesis.

claims.rationale: This is one of the strongest empirical refutations of Illig's hypothesis. Chinese astronomical records of Halley's Comet (and other comets, eclipses, and sunspots) form an unbroken sequence from 240 BC onward, and the recorded apparitions during 614-911 AD (e.g., 684, 760, 837, 912 AD) match modern orbital computations precisely. Phantom Time would require these dated Chinese observations to have been impossibly rebackdated.

dependencies.rationale: Independent astronomical chronology refutes the missing-centuries claim.

claims.text: The apparent paucity of European Dark-Age records is not evidence of fabrication but the predictable consequence of Roman infrastructure collapse — almost 95% of the world's population was in poverty, less than 5% were literate, so written records and architectural achievement naturally thinned out.

claims.rationale: The mainstream historical explanation: post-Roman collapse, mass illiteracy, and infrastructure decay account for the relative thinness of Western European 7th-9th-century documentary record without any need to invoke a conspiracy. The 95% poverty / 5% literacy figures are loose but in the right ballpark for the early medieval period.

dependencies.rationale: Provides a mundane explanation for the documentary gap that Illig treats as evidence of fabrication.

claims.text: Pope Gregory's 10-day calendar correction was mathematically correct because it was anchored to the 325 AD Council of Nicaea (not to 45 BC) — Nicaea had already shifted Easter calculations by 3 days, so 1257 years of Julian drift since Nicaea required exactly the 10-day jump that Gregory implemented; no missing centuries are needed to explain the math.

claims.rationale: Correct on the math and the chronology. Gregory's bull Inter gravissimas (1582) explicitly anchors the correction to Nicaea (325) rather than to the original Julian adoption. 325 → 1582 = 1257 years; with one-day drift per 128 years that's 9.82 days, which Gregory rounded to 10. The arithmetic-conspiracy argument that drives Illig's hypothesis dissolves once the correct anchor date is used.

dependencies.rationale: Resolves the apparent 3-day discrepancy without needing missing centuries.

claims.text: The vast majority of professional historians consider the Phantom Time Hypothesis debunked, and the host concurs — while Illig and a small group continue to defend it, the cumulative weight of astronomical chronology, archaeology, and recalculated calendar arithmetic falsifies the conspiracy.

claims.rationale: This is the host's verdict and it matches academic consensus. The hypothesis is treated by working medievalists as pseudohistory. The host's framing accurately summarizes the situation.
